1: 'At that time ... the bones of the kings of Judah, the bones of its officials, the bones of the priests, the bones of the prophets, and the bones of Jerusalem's inhabitants will be brought out of their graves' — the desecration of the royal tombs is one of the most extreme forms of posthumous judgment imaginable in the ANE. Kings were buried with honor in dynastic tombs (the tombs of David: 1 Kings 2:10); disruption of royal burials was practiced by invaders as a sign of complete domination. The specific categories — kings, officials, priests, prophets, inhabitants — correspond exactly to the leadership classes indicted in 2:8 and Jeremiah 7:26. The judgment is comprehensive across all social strata.
2: 'They will be exposed before the sun, the moon, and all the host of heaven — which this people loved, served, followed, consulted, and worshipped' — the five-verb indictment for astral worship is the most concentrated description of star-cult devotion in the OT. The five verbs (loved, served, followed, consulted, worshipped) describe different aspects of the relationship with astral deities: emotional attachment (ʾāhab), active service (ʿābad), lifestyle orientation (hālak ʾaḥărê), inquiry (dāraš), and prostration (šāḥāh). The ironic punishment is that the bones will be exposed before the very celestial objects the people worshipped — but now as refuse, not as devotees.
3: 'And all the survivors of this evil family who remain in all the places where I have driven them will prefer death to life' — the desire for death over life reverses the life-orientation of the covenant: YHWH offered 'life and prosperity' (Deuteronomy 30:15), and the covenant people are now choosing their opposites. The survivors in the diaspora will have witnessed such devastation that continued existence offers nothing — the annihilation of their entire social world (temple, land, community, institutions) makes death preferable to the meaningless survival of a covenant people without their covenant context.
4: 'When people fall, don't they get back up? When someone turns away, don't they come back?' — the wisdom sayings contrast natural human resilience with Israel's theological stubbornness. When a person stumbles (yippōl), they naturally rise (yāqûm); when they turn away (yāšûb), they naturally return (yāšûb). The wordplay is intentional: the 'returning' that characterizes normal human behavior is precisely what Israel refuses to do in its covenant context. Repentance (šûbāh) is treated as less demanding than the automatic physical response to falling — and yet Israel will not do it.
5: 'Then why has this people — Jerusalem — turned away in perpetual backsliding? They cling to deception and refuse to come back' — mĕšûbāh niṣṣaḥat (perpetual/eternal backsliding) is Jeremiah's diagnostic: the turning away has become a permanent orientation rather than a temporary failure. 'Clinging to deception' (heḥĕzîqû bammirmāh) uses the same verb ḥāzaq (to hold fast/cling) used for Israel's covenant loyalty to YHWH in positive contexts (Deuteronomy 10:20; Ruth 1:14) — the irony is that the clinging-loyalty that should be directed toward YHWH is now directed toward the deceptions that substitute for him.
6: 'No one repents of his wickedness and asks, What have I done?' — the absence of the diagnostic question 'What have I done?' is the sign of moral self-awareness destroyed. The horse-plunging-into-battle image is vivid: warhorses trained for cavalry combat charge without deliberation, driven by training and adrenaline. Israel's moral behavior has become similarly automatic and unreflective — each person charges ahead on their own course (šāqap bĕmĕrûṣātô) without pausing to assess direction or destination. The absence of moral deliberation is itself a form of moral pathology.
7: 'Even the stork in the sky knows when to migrate; the turtledove, the swallow, and the crane observe the season of their return — but my people do not know the requirements of the LORD' — the migratory bird comparison is a form of shame rhetoric: the birds follow their divinely-embedded instincts with precision; Israel ignores its divinely-given covenant. The four birds listed were familiar to the Levantine climate as seasonal migrants. Mišpāṭ Yhwh (the requirements/ordinances of the LORD) refers to the covenant norms that the Torah made explicit — Israel's failure is not lack of information but lack of attentiveness to what the birds instinctively know: the right season for the right response.
8: 'How can you claim, We are wise, and we have the law of the LORD? But in fact the lying pen of the scribes has turned it into a lie' — the 'lying pen of the scribes' (ʿēṭ šeqer sōpĕrîm) is a critique of the scribal interpretation tradition that was producing Torah commentary and legal rulings in Jeremiah's era. Whether this refers to literal forgery (scholars debate) or to interpretive distortion that created legal loopholes from Torah requirements, the indictment is that the written law (tôrat Yhwh) was being undermined by the very specialists who claimed to preserve and transmit it. The critique anticipates Jesus's conflicts with scribal tradition (Matthew 15:6: 'you have made void the word of God by your tradition').
9: 'The wise will be disgraced; they will be terrified and caught. They have rejected the word of the LORD — so what wisdom is left in them?' — the ḥăkāmîm (wise men/sages) were a distinct professional class in ancient Israel, distinct from the priests and prophets (cf. Jeremiah 18:18: 'the law shall not perish from the priest, nor counsel from the wise, nor the word from the prophet'). Wisdom in the OT begins with the fear of the LORD (Proverbs 1:7); those who reject the LORD's word have destroyed the foundation of the very wisdom they claim. The categorical question 'what wisdom is left in them?' rhetorically answers itself: none.
10: 'Therefore I will give their wives to other men and their fields to new owners, because from the least to the greatest all are consumed by greed; from prophet to priest, all deal dishonestly' — the loss of wives and fields represents the total dispossession of the household unit — the fundamental social structure of Israelite life. The universal greedy indictment (miqqāṭōn wĕʿad-gādôl kullō bōṣēaʿ bāṣaʿ) from least to greatest covers the entire social spectrum. The prophet-to-priest inclusion of religious leaders in the indictment mirrors the comprehensive leadership failure in 2:8 and Jeremiah 5:31.
11: 'They dress the wound of my people as though it were minor, saying, Peace, peace, when there is no peace' — the 'peace, peace' (šālôm šālôm) false promise is one of the most memorable phrases in Jeremiah, repeated at 6:14 and echoed throughout the book's conflict with false prophets. Šālôm as covenant completeness — the comprehensive well-being that YHWH promises to the obedient community — has been misappropriated as a political-religious slogan assuring Jerusalem that no judgment is coming. The wound metaphor (šeber, the fracture/break of my people) suggests a structural injury; the false prophets are applying ointment to what requires surgery.
12: 'Were they ashamed when they committed these detestable acts? No, they felt no shame at all; they did not even know how to blush' — the incapacity for shame (hikkālēm lōʾ yēdāʿû) is Jeremiah's diagnosis of the social-psychological consequence of long-established sin: the moral feedback mechanism (blushing as the involuntary sign of conscience) has been destroyed by habituation to wrong. The parallel at 6:15 connects this shame-incapacity to the confident leadership of prophets and priests who will fall in the time of punishment.
13: 'I will take away their harvest, declares the LORD: no grapes on the vine, no figs on the fig tree, even the leaves will wither' — the agricultural curse of stripped vines and barren fig trees reverses the covenant blessing symbols. The vine and fig tree together represent the standard ANE image of peaceful agricultural plenty (1 Kings 4:25; Micah 4:4; Zechariah 3:10); their barrenness is the visible sign of covenant-curse. 'What I gave them will be stripped away' (wāʾettēn lāhem yaʿabĕrûm, lit. 'those I gave to them will pass away') — the gifts of the land return to YHWH when the land-lease conditions are violated.
14: 'Why are we sitting here? Come, let us flee to the fortified cities and die there — for the LORD our God has condemned us and has given us poisoned water to drink, because we have sinned against the LORD' — the community voice of defeat anticipates the siege of Jerusalem. The 'sitting here' implies paralysis in the face of advancing Babylonian forces; the flight to fortified cities is the desperate last resort. 'Condemned us' (hĕdimmānû) and 'poisoned water' (mê rōʾš, water of gall/poison) are the language of the covenant curse enacted — the people recognize that their suffering is covenantally deserved, a recognition that does not produce repentance but only fatalistic acceptance.
15: 'We hoped for peace, but no good has come; we waited for a time of healing, but instead there is only terror' — the reversal of the false prophets' promise: the community hoped for what the false prophets promised and received the opposite. This is the experiential confirmation of Jeremiah's charge against the 'peace, peace' slogan — the people who trusted the false prophets discover, in the moment of judgment, that the promise was empty. Rĕpāʾût (healing) is the covenant-restoration term; the 'time of healing' that has not come will be promised genuinely in 30:17 and Jeremiah 33:6.
16: 'From Dan the snorting of their warhorses is heard; the whole land shakes at the sound of their stallions' neighing' — Dan was the northernmost Israelite city (modern Tel Dan), the first to be reached by an invasion from the north (the Babylonian route from Mesopotamia via the Fertile Crescent). The battle sounds starting from Dan function as an alarm that reaches the whole land progressively. The horse imagery for the Babylonian cavalry is vivid — war chariots and cavalry were the decisive military technology of the Neo-Babylonian army.
17: 'For I am about to send poisonous snakes among you — vipers that cannot be charmed — and they will bite you, declares the LORD' — the snake-that-cannot-be-charmed image builds on the 'poisoned water' (rōʾš = poison/gall) theme of Jeremiah 8:14. Snake-charming was a recognized practice in the ancient Near East (cf. Psalms 58:4-5; Ecclesiastes 10:11); the vipers that resist charming are invaders whose advance cannot be neutralized by any means — neither diplomatic negotiation nor magical protection. The image connects to Numbers 21:6 (YHWH sending fiery serpents against Israel in the wilderness) — the same divine agent who once used serpents to discipline Israel uses them again.
18: 'My sorrow is beyond healing; my heart is faint within me' — the shift from divine oracle to prophetic lament is sudden and significant. Jeremiah speaks in his own voice about his personal grief over the coming judgment he has been proclaiming. The 'sorrow beyond healing' (yaʾgôn ʿālay libbî dāwwāy) reflects the prophet's unique position: he knows the judgment is justified and coming, but he is not emotionally detached from the people who will suffer it. Jeremiah is often described as 'the weeping prophet' because of passages like this, Jeremiah 9:1, and the Lamentations attributed to him.
19: 'Listen — the cry of my people from a distant land: Is the LORD not in Zion? Is her King no longer there?' — the distant-land cry is the voice of exile before it has happened: Jeremiah hears in prophetic vision the anguished questions of a dispersed people asking whether the exile has voided the Zion covenant. 'Is her King no longer there?' — ʾên malkāh bāh — directly challenges the Davidic kingship theology. YHWH's inserted counter-question ('Why have they provoked me with their carved idols and worthless foreign gods?') answers the cry with the reason for the divine absence: idolatry caused the very abandonment the people now lament.
20: 'The harvest is over, the summer has ended — and we are still not saved' — this is among the most poignant lines in Jeremiah: the agricultural seasons have passed, the opportunities for action have run out, and salvation has not come. In Palestinian agriculture, the grain harvest (spring) and the summer fruit harvest (August-September) together constituted the year's productive capacity; when both were over, the year was finished. The seasonal metaphor expresses a theological reality: the time for repentance and covenant restoration has closed, and the people are still in their unresolved crisis. Paul may have this image in mind with 'now is the acceptable time' (2 Corinthians 6:2).
21: 'I am broken over the brokenness of my people; I mourn, and horror has gripped me' — ʿal-šeber bat-ʿammî hušbārtî (I am broken over the breaking of my people's daughter) — Jeremiah participates in the very judgment he has announced. The word šeber (breaking, fracture) is used for the mortal wound that will bring Judah down; Jeremiah suffers that wound vicariously. This is not professional detachment but pastoral solidarity: the prophet's grief is proportionate to the scale of the disaster. Lamentations 2:11 uses similar language in Jeremiah's voice over the fall of Jerusalem.
22: 'Is there no balm in Gilead? Is there no physician there? Then why has the wound of my people not been healed?' — Gilead (Transjordan) was famous in antiquity for its balsam resin (ṣĕrî), used as a wound-dressing and healing agent (Genesis 37:25: Joseph sold by the Midianites who were carrying Gileadite balm to Egypt). The three rhetorical questions are saturated with grief: the resources exist (balm, physician), yet the healing has not come. The answer — implied but not stated — is that Israel's wound is not physical but covenantal, and the covenantal physician is being refused. The image inspired the American spiritual 'There Is a Balm in Gilead,' which applies the image christologically: the healing that is not available through the law is available through the gospel.
